title: Switchable Surface Linear Photogalvanic Effect in the Magnetic Weyl Semimetal Co3Sn2S2
authors: Niket Shah, Aymen Nomani, Kai Chen, Hridis Pal, Pavan Hosur
abstract: We investigate the linear photogalvanic effect (LPGE) on the surface of the magnetic Weyl semimetal Co3Sn2S2 using a Green's-function and diagrammatic formalism. While the LPGE vanishes in the centrosymmetric bulk, it is symmetry-allowed on the surface where inversion symmetry is broken. We show that unitary crystal symmetries on the surface produce characteristic sign reversals of the total photocurrent at certain polarization angles upon flipping the magnetization. We further find that the intrinsic contribution to the LPGE is strongly constrained by an antiunitary mirror symmetry, which forces several nonlinear response tensor elements to vanish. In contrast, the extrinsic contribution is not subject to these constraints and displays a large magnitude which, we argue, is due to the enhanced density of states associated with Fermi-arc surface states. The current exhibits an approximately linear temperature dependence and a low-frequency power-law scaling, |jy| proportional to omega^-2.2, with weak temperature dependence of the scaling exponent. Our results identify Co3Sn2S2 as a promising platform for experimentally accessing symmetry-controlled nonlinear transport in realistic systems and for applications in magnetically controlled optoelectronic devices.
summary: This paper theoretically demonstrates how the surface of the magnetic Weyl semimetal Co3Sn2S2 enables a switchable linear photogalvanic effect through magnetization control. The researchers show that the response is driven by surface Fermi arcs and follows a specific power-law frequency scaling. These findings suggest the material is a strong candidate for developing new magnetically controlled optoelectronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating the origin, symmetry constraints, and magnitude of the surface Linear Photogalvanic Effect (LPGE) in the magnetic Weyl semimetal Co3Sn2S2.
main_result: The study finds that the LPGE is localized to the surface due to broken inversion symmetry and that the extrinsic contribution is large due to enhanced density of states from Fermi arcs. It also identifies a power-law frequency scaling of |jy| proportional to omega^-2.2 and a magnetization-induced sign reversal.
method: A Green's-function and diagrammatic formalism is used to evaluate the second-order response function via four Feynman diagrams.
model_or_system: The system is the magnetic kagome Weyl semimetal Co3Sn2S2, modeled using a multi-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian on a 40-layer slab to capture surface inversion symmetry breaking.
key_observables: Linear photogalvanic effect (LPGE) currents (jx, jy), Circular photogalvanic effect (CPGE) currents, and the second-order response tensor elements.
important_parameters: Magnetization direction (m), polarization angle (phi), frequency (omega), temperature (T), and scattering rate (delta).
main_assumptions: The calculation assumes the response originates from the surface and uses an effective tight-binding Hamiltonian derived from previous literature.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows Feynman diagrams for the second-order response; Fig 2b illustrates the rhombohedral lattice structure; Fig 5 shows polar plots of photocurrents as a function of polarization angle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of LPGE in Co3Sn2S2, details the tight-binding model and diagrammatic formalism, analyzes symmetry constraints on the response tensor, presents results regarding frequency and temperature dependence, and concludes with implications for optoelectronic applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The four Feynman diagrams used to calculate χµαβ. The dotted lines represent the output current j while the wavy solid lines represent external fields.

FIG. 2. Effective structure of Co3Sn2S2 with the S-layer and the Sn-atoms in the Co layer ignored along with the experi- mental geometry. (a) Kagome structure of the Co layer and the position of the triangular Sn layer relative to the Co layer in the x-y plane. (b) Tetrahedral unit cell formed by the Sn atoms and the lattice translation vectors ⃗ai. (c) Schematic showing the layered structure of Co3Sn2S2 with a uniform ac electric field ⃗E applied to the slab at polarization angle ϕ.

FIG. 3. LPGE currents jy are plotted as a function of temperature T for an applied electric field amplitude |E| = 105 V/m and magnetization strength J|m| = 0.18 eV. The polarization angle of the incident electric field and the magnetization direction are indicated in the captions of the individual panels. The currents for ϕ = 0 are calculated from the conductivities using the expression jy = χyxx|E|2 and those for ϕ = π/2 are calculated using jy = χyyy|E|2. The lines represent linear fit of the form ax + b. Solid lines represent linear fit for the data corresponding to ˆm = +ˆz, while dotted lines represent data for ˆm = −ˆz Insets display the frequency dependence of the extracted slope a....

FIG. 4. jy vs ω for an applied electric field amplitude |E| = 105 V/m and magnetization strength J|m| = 0.18 eV. Since the currents in panels (b) and (c) are negative, −jy is plotted as indicated in the y labels. The polarization angle of the incident electric field and the magnetization direction are indicated in the titles of the individual panels. The currents for ϕ = 0 are calculated from the conductivities using the expression jy = χyxx|E|2 and those for ϕ = π/2 are calculated using jy = χyyy|E|2. Solid lines represent linear fits of the form log |jy| = a log ω + b. Insets display the temperature dependence of the extracted slope a. The plot demonstrates a power law scaling of the...

FIG. 5. Polar plots of LPGE currents vs the polarization angle ϕ of a linearly polarized electric field for magnetization ˆm = ±ˆz. Parameters used are |E| = 105 V/m, J|m| = 0.18 eV, ℏω = 0.15 eV, and T = 87 K.
